author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: The Cultivation Claims can be used in Faction Claims right now, generating R4 proportional to their Claim Stake counterpart (tier). I think, at a minimum, we will be able to support CC resource production with the mainnet release of C4. Closing the loop is the only outstanding question I have internally. Which is to say, will we all be able to produce the consumables that actually apply buffs with the release? I’m not totally sure. But I think it’s likely feasible depending on the bug fixing work the team will need to prioritize

reply.preview: The Cultivation Claims can be used in Faction Claims right now, generating R4 proportional to their Claim Stake counterpart (tier). I think, at a minimum, we will be able to support CC resource production with the mainnet release of C4. Closing the loop is the only outstanding question I have internally. Which is to say, will we all be able to produce the consumables that actually apply buffs with the release? I’m not totally sure. author: Dinarei
content: I have a question related to the exploration aspect of the game. In the PTR, we have immediate access to all the geological data of all the asteroid and planets. And all the planets of a given type within a faction seem to have the same resources, but with different richness. Is there a plan to add the exploration loops later and get more variety across planets? Like being able to scan planets, get geological maps that can be traded. Maybe find anomalies on certain planets, that kind of things? I imagine simple game loops like Stellaris, where the resources of each planet is unknown until scanning and the data runners can also help detecting anomalies.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Given all the data lives onchain, it is unlikely that we can make resources discoverable, in much the same way that we can't concretely introduce a fog of war on player fleets. We could hide them in the UI, but it would be trivial to look them up in the blockchain data. However, I'm double checking on this for you.

reply.preview: Given all the data lives onchain, it is unlikely that we can make resources discoverable, in much the same way that we can't concretely introduce a fog of war on player fleets. We could hide them in the UI, but it would be trivial to look them up in the blockchain data. However, I'm double checking on this for you.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: IMO it would largely be superficial to implement given all resources would be revealed within a short window of time (probably a week or so).

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I think that was because each SAGE account has its own player profile, which is what we used to derive zProfiles. And your player profile becomes it's own primary zProfile. However, I also thought we fixed that so you could delink a SAGE profile as a primary profile. When was the last time you tried?

reply.preview: I think that was because each SAGE account has its own player profile, which is what we used to derive zProfiles. And your player profile becomes it's own primary zProfile. However, I also thought we fixed that so you could delink a SAGE profile as a primary profile. When was the last time you tried?

author: Brunn [BDGR]
content:
In a conservative scenario, based on the number of Steam reviews, you can estimate that SpaceCraft has already sold around 30,000 copies — and that's in just 4 days since launch.

The game is rough and limited, let's be honest. But even so, it's having relative success within the space game niche. And the reason is the most basic thing in the world: it does the simple stuff well. You jump in, mine, build, fly, automate, and have fun.

And looking at that "simple that just works," it brings back the same question I've been asking for years: when is Star Atlas finally going to start focusing on the actual game (the Unreal version) and make running everything on the blockchain secondary?

Don't get me wrong — I'm 100% on board, and I think it's fantastic having my assets as NFTs and the in-game currency as a real crypto. But wanting to run everything on-chain is a bet that goes beyond my ability to predict how it'll play out.

Meanwhile, a "rough" game like SpaceCraft will probably blow past the 100k mark just by doing the basics.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: Steam is undoubtedly the best distribution channel for games. It's unfortunate they have not taken a more progressive approach to web3. Coincidentally, we were just discussing this morning some options we have to launch product(s) through Steam, which could consist of isolated game modes from the UE client, as well as our campaign mode (single player) for C4. It's viable, and could also create alternative revenue streams for us. However, these products would all have to be completely stripped of their web3 elements. Not necessarily a bad thing. Current thinking is that, beyond the revenue, it could be really great for visibility. And we obviously have no intention of abandoning our web3 efforts. We're currently assessing the workload in producing those individual clients. All that said, I would probably emphasize that we aren't currently in a capital position to really focus on UE work. It's extremely expensive, and we don't have the same opportunities to incorporate agentic development into that pipeline like we do with web development. Though I should not overstate that, because we haven't fully explored the options on that side yet with much of our effort being dedicated to the release of C4.

I can't understand its meaning or value

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: @SW4GN3R How realistic/doable is it to strip the blockchain part from UE5, put it on Steam in early access for revenue, and when the timing is right, add the blockchain stuff? We already have assets for a simple single-player quest game + multiplayer for racing and shooting, if I'm not mistaken.

author: Brunn [BDGR]
content: Before ask any treasury withdrawal, shouldn't the team present a concrete plan for how they intend to make the project profitable or attractive to investors? The proposal is framed as a reimbursement for past costs, but in practice this money will be used to keep the project running.  So...  I'd much rather vote on a clear plan to make Star Atlas profitable than on a blank check.

author: Brunn [BDGR]
content: Well, beyond all this discussion about the treasury, funding, and the game's direction, I'd suggest putting together a Plan B in case the funding runs out: a framework to make the project open source, with a voting model similar to Bitcoin's — but 80/20 or 70/30 instead of 90/10. This could even extend to the UE5 project through mod support, where community-built mods could later be incorporated into the "vanilla" game, also by DAO vote.

author: Brunn [BDGR]
content: We're currently assessing a few avenues, so I can't really accurately respond yet. But as Funcracker said, we have limited blockchain integration into UE right now, so that piece is theoretically pretty easy to strip out. I also discussed this on the Brew yesterday, but @bunthius has a functioning version of C4 single player (campaign) mode already. Ran a /goal with Codex over 8 days it and fully rebuilt C4 offchain. Pretty amazing.  Also under consideration is whether or not we want to release UE products as individual dedicated features (racing, arena, Galia), or as a consolidated early access game. @ROME | Winston commented on that, and we are leaning more towards a consolidated product release. Isolating UE features into a dedicated release would be a much bigger release, and then we'd have 4-5 clients we need to maintain.  With that said, I'm not entirely confident that we have enough content to create sticky engagement. We do have pretty cool progression tracks, and a ton of art assets that would be included with the release. All offchain of course, and no resale value. But progression that could be attractive to mainstream gamers. And we would be targeting a low price point between $5-$15 for early access. These are the ideas we're kicking around. Still lower priority to our C4 efforts. But as I noted earlier, could be a decent supplemental revenue stream, and more importantly, drive visibility to Star Atlas.

Pretty amazing. Still lower priority to our C4 efforts. But as I noted earlier, could be a decent supplemental revenue stream, and more importantly, drive visibility to Star Atlas.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I paid $45 for Star Citizen back in 2016. There was less content than Star Atlas UE5 has right now, but there was focus on the game and constant updates — which is exactly what got me to spend a lot more in the years that followed. With Star Atlas, though, it's been a long time since I've seen my assets progress. I don't see new ships entering the game. I don't see ships advancing through their development stages. I don't see new mechanics, or new places to visit. The value of an Early Access goes beyond the current product… it's also about trust in delivery and in regular updates. Asking for $5 doesn't seem like a project with a future to me.

Asking for $5 doesn't seem like a project with a future to me.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Hey everyone! Long time no see. Sorry for being MIA, but it's a pretty intense period for me..

I think the key words here are exactly repeatable loops. They give you a good balance between development effort and enjoyable gameplay. I am thinking of the Battlefront II style gameplay we talked when Danny presented LAR4, or maybe procedurally generated maps that players can enter and loot, similar to an extraction shooter (ARC Raiders).

I know the situation, and I am not suggesting we should throw money at the UE5 module, unless AI with the UE MCP server works miracles.

Look at the new game Fatekeeper. It has hyper realistic graphics, and it is really impressive. It costs $8 in Early Access and offers around two hours of gameplay. If you ask $10 for LAR4, Exinade, and a new gameplay loop with a Battlefront or Arc Raiders style, I think you could reasonably ask for $10 to $15.

I would not release Galia yet, because it still needs some polish. Brad is doing outstanding work there, but I feel that it should be our ace up our sleeve, the jaw dropping update once it is more polished (new locomotion system for example is one of the polish I'm thinking of and it should be pretty close right?).

Just my two cents, of course!

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: @bunthius probably has much deeper thoughts on the whole thing

author: bunthius [STAR]
content: Definitely have a lot of thoughts, you guys are all covering them the same way. I bought fatekeeper on their EA launch too, subnautica 2 as well. There's certainly some interesting psychology around EA and people even just picking it up for a perceived discount to own it when the full game is completed. Just recent a few long term, high profile EA games have been hitting 1.0 too, which is helpful for that strategy in building trust unlike the opposite of what Kickstarter did. And stuff like Ashes of Creation having to forfeit some sales because the promises didn't align with their shutdown. I think there's a way forward here with the amount of content we have, it's not very different than a lot of other games going into EA who have even less content. There's a lot of stuff that we didn't fully hook up yet as well, insane amount of content. I don't see too much polish need to get to a very viable EA game. As long as we hit the perceived value amount of content and play time for the cost then people will see the value. But we're not rushing into this pivot and will need to add some polish before a release there. Just having the visibility there will be a fun moment. Even if we have to absorb the tsunami of crypto have for a game that will have zero crypto lol.

author: bunthius [STAR]
content: I want to show people how much has been built, it's VERY substantial and can go toe to toe with a lot of other games making revenue. Also, we'll see how some polish on Galia can occur before then, but yeah, this idea is to remove all experimental content.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
I believe EA on Steam should be part of a larger campaign, a bridge between the Web3 and Web2 worlds. Having a client on the Epic Store was one of the strong selling points for SA previously. Releasing a demo on Steam could be a strong boost at the right moment of the market. Considering the 30% Valve commission and the risks associated with traditional players' initial pessimism toward crypto projects (which could result in review bombing if they see anything less than GTA 6 after spending their $10), simply releasing the game on Steam could be a risky move. Keep in mind it is one time shot most of the times. Not many projects have recovered from a failed launch.

However, with polished project Galia, fixed bugs in the existing modes, and also transfer Holosim to the game client, the release of such demo could become the necessary catalyst for attracting attention and new players to c4.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Why does every TPS replicate Fortnite movement

reply.preview: Why does every TPS replicate Fortnite movement

author: Kr1gs
content: Because most teams, us included, use Lyra for the motion/movement system out the box. We have put considerable work into custom motion matching though, and I’m looking forward to seeing it implemented. But to answer your question, it’s because teams use default movement engine.

reply.preview: Because most teams, us included, use Lyra for the motion/movement system out the box. We have put considerable work into custom motion matching though, and I’m looking forward to seeing it implemented. But to answer your question, it’s because teams use default movement engine.
